content_type_hook: Open on a dresser top or bathroom counter at surface level. A collection of bangles is the subject. Creator moving in background, out of focus. Her voice off-camera: 'I started doing it in 2008. I told myself I was going to buy myself something every year that was just for me.'
why_it_works: The annual self-gifting ritual is one of the most under-documented consumer behaviors in the jewelry category — brands rarely address the woman who buys for herself with intention and consistency. This concept makes that behavior visible and gives it language ('the ritual is the reason'), validating a practice that many customers do privately and slightly guiltily. The distinction 'not who I was — who I was trying to be' is philosophically aligned with Alex and Ani's empowerment tone at its deepest level: the brand is not documenting who you are, it is tracking who you are becoming.

concept.title: The Annual Reminder

storyboard.visual: Camera at dresser or bathroom counter surface level — collection of bangles in the foreground, slightly out of focus. Woman moves in the background, unhurried, not yet in frame. The bangles are the subject before she is.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Her voice off-camera, easy and slightly private: 'I started doing it in 2008. I told myself I was going to buy myself something every year that was just for me. Not for anyone else. Just — mine.'

storyboard.visual: She enters frame — sits or stands at the counter, picks up bangles one by one without hurrying. Her manner is familiar with these objects. Camera stays at counter level.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Her voice: 'The first year I almost didn't do it. I thought, who am I to spend money on myself.' A real pause. 'I did it anyway.'

storyboard.visual: She holds up the first bracelet — worn, clearly old. Camera moves to a close macro on it in her hand. Text overlay appears.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Text overlay: 'Tree of Life — 2008.' Her voice: 'I wore it every day for three years straight. And then I sent it in for repair.' Slight laugh. 'When it came back I put it on and — I don't know. It was like seeing someone after a long time.'

storyboard.visual: Back to her face — she is easier now, more fluid. Her wrist is visible and she gestures while talking. The bangles on her wrist move as she speaks. Camera is warmer here, slightly tighter.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Her voice: 'In 2015 I picked the Hamsa because that was the year I finally started saying no to things. In 2019 I picked the arrow — forward momentum — I had just gone back to school at 43.' Small pause. 'I did finish.' Text overlays: 'Hamsa — 2015 / Arrow — 2019' appear briefly as she names each.

storyboard.visual: Quick bracelet inserts — macro shots of specific named pieces, each 2-3 seconds. Returns to her face. Music enters barely — single acoustic guitar, two sustained notes, no melody yet. She looks directly at camera for the first time.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Her voice, directly to camera: 'I have seventeen now. And when I look at them I can remember who I was trying to be that year. Not who I was. Who I was trying to be.' A beat of silence. 'That's different.' Single acoustic guitar — two notes only, barely audible.

storyboard.visual: She picks up the most recent bracelet from the collection — holds it up briefly, then slides it on her wrist. Close-up on wrist and hand as it settles into place alongside the other bangles.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Her voice, quieter: 'This one I just got. I don't know what it means yet. That's okay. It'll tell me.' Guitar resolves to a single final note.

storyboard.visual: Cut to pure black. Clean serif typography: '2004–2026.' No logo animation. Guitar resolves to one final note — then silence. 3-second hold.
storyboard.audio_or_text: Single guitar note, resolving. Then silence. Text overlay: '2004–2026.' No CTA. No URL. Fade.

concept.copy: Every January since 2008, she buys herself one. She doesn't need a reason anymore. The ritual is the reason.
concept.reference: Inspired by Glossier's 'Into the Gloss' editorial interview format and the confessional-to-camera grammar of early 'Top Shelf' videos — adapted by grounding the ritual in a product collection rather than a beauty routine, and by drawing the emotional peak to a philosophical distinction ('who I was trying to be, not who I was') rather than a product revelation. Pinterest secondary placement strategy draws from Rifle Paper Co.'s success with curated collection content on the platform.
concept.product_placement_notes: All bangles shown are the subject's own real pieces — critical for authenticity. The most recent bracelet (Scene 6) should be the only new or nearly new piece in the collection. Named pieces (Tree of Life 2008, Hamsa 2015, Arrow 2019) should be identified visually and via text overlay but need not be seeded — if they are not in her actual collection, do not fabricate them. The collection as a whole is the product statement — individual products are chapters, not heroes. No seeded product introduction.
concept.talent_direction: Woman, age 35-52. Must be an actual Alex and Ani customer since 2007-2014, with documented purchase pattern of 10+ transactions spread across 10+ years, clustering in Q1. Source via direct email to this purchase-data segment with honest ask: seeking someone who does this privately. The slight initial embarrassment ('who am I to spend money on myself') dissolving into clarity ('I did it anyway') is the emotional engine — casting must find someone for whom this arc is genuine, not performed. Real home setting, real dresser or jewelry surface. Wardrobe: casual home clothes. The line 'Not who I was — who I was trying to be' is the singular creative centerpiece.
